title: CMMC Assessment Process (CAP)
core_principle: The CCP assists. The CCA assesses. The Lead Assessor leads. The CCP never makes independent assessment determinations.

roles_on_assessment_team.CCP: Assists the assessment team across all four phases. Collects and examines evidence, supports scoring, helps prepare reports. Cannot make independent scoring decisions.
roles_on_assessment_team.CCA: Certified CMMC Assessor. Conducts the assessment and makes scoring determinations.
roles_on_assessment_team.Lead_Assessor: Leads the assessment team. Makes final scoring decisions. Drafts and submits the final assessment report.

phases.name: Plan and Prepare
phases.summary: Everything that happens before the assessment starts.

tasks.description: Review the OSC's environment, identify which practices apply, understand what evidence will be needed. CCP assists in reviewing documentation and understanding system boundaries.
Formal document defining assessment scope, practices being assessed, methodology, logistics, team roles, and how evidence will be collected and handled. CCP assists under the Lead Assessor.

Verifies the OSC is actually prepared before the formal assessment begins. Protects both parties — OSC doesn't burn their assessment on unpreparedness, team doesn't waste time on an unprepared environment. If OSC isn't ready, assessment does not proceed.

phases.CCP_role: Assist in analyzing requirements, assist in developing assessment plan, assist in verifying readiness.

phases.name: Conduct Assessment
phases.summary: The actual assessment. Evidence collected, practices scored, preliminary results validated.

tasks.description: Gather evidence for each practice using Examine, Interview, and Test methods. CCP assists in collecting and examining evidence — pulling documentation, sitting in on interviews, observing demonstrations.

tasks.task: Score practices and validate preliminary results
tasks.description: Each practice scored MET, NOT MET, or N/A based on evidence. Team validates preliminary results together. One NOT MET objective = entire practice NOT MET. No partial credit.
tasks.critical_rule: Practices on a POA&M are scored NOT MET during Phase 2. POA&M evaluation happens in Phase 4, not here.

tasks.description: Team produces final set of findings. Lead Assessor makes final scoring decisions. CCP assists but does not make independent scoring calls.

phases.CCP_role: Assist in collecting and examining evidence. Assist in scoring practices and validating preliminary results. Assist in generating final assessment results. Cannot make independent assessment determinations.

phases.name: Report Assessment Results
phases.summary: Findings documented, delivered, submitted, and archived.

tasks.description: Lead Assessor drafts and scores final findings based on validated preliminary results from Phase 2. CCP assists in preparing report content.
OSC receives preliminary results before the final report is officially submitted. Gives OSC visibility into findings before anything is recorded.
Lead Assessor submits final report to both the C3PAO and the OSC. C3PAO reviews before anything is submitted to SPRS.

All evidence, findings, and documentation packaged and archived to support any future questions about the assessment. Hashing creates cryptographic fingerprints of evidence at time of collection — proves integrity if anyone later questions whether evidence was altered.

submission_chain: Lead Assessor drafts and scores final findings
Lead Assessor submits report to C3PAO and OSC
phases.CCP_role: Assist in delivering recommended assessment results. Assist in packaging and archiving assessment results.

phases.name: POA&M Evaluation
phases.summary: Evaluation of outstanding NOT MET practices to determine if conditional certification is possible.

key_terms.POA&M: Plan of Action and Milestones. Formal document created when practices are scored NOT MET during assessment. Identifies the gap, explains how it will be fixed, and sets milestones for remediation.
key_terms.Conditional_certification: Allows an OSC to operate while remediating NOT MET practices rather than failing outright and losing contract eligibility. A safety valve, not a loophole.
key_terms.Operational_plan_of_action: The OSC's internal document for managing temporary deficiencies outside of a formal assessment context. NOT the same as an assessment POA&M.

qualifying_criteria.description: OSC must score above a minimum threshold even with NOT MET practices. If too far below it, conditional certification is not available — remediation required first.
Not every NOT MET practice qualifies. The item must be remediable — a fix exists and is achievable within a defined timeframe. Structural gaps with no viable path to remediation do not qualify.
POA&M must include specific milestones demonstrating real movement toward remediation. Vague commitments do not qualify.

phases.CCP_role: Awareness of the POA&M process. Support evaluation of POA&M items. Cannot make final determinations on whether items qualify.

POA&M_vs_operational_plan_of_action.POA&M: Associated with a formal CMMC assessment. Created when practices score NOT MET. Reviewed by assessment team in Phase 4.
POA&M_vs_operational_plan_of_action.Operational_plan_of_action: OSC's internal document for managing temporary deficiencies outside formal assessment. Not the same as assessment POA&M.

key_distinctions.hashing_purpose: Hashing proves evidence integrity — creates a tamper-evident seal, not an access control mechanism. If evidence is questioned later, re-hash and compare to prove it was not altered.

flashcards.question: What are the four phases of the CMMC Assessment Process?
flashcards.answer: Phase 1: Plan and Prepare. Phase 2: Conduct Assessment. Phase 3: Report Results. Phase 4: POA&M Evaluation.

flashcards.question: What is the CCP's role across all four CAP phases in plain terms?
flashcards.answer: The CCP assists. Always. Across all four phases the CCP supports the assessment team but never leads and never makes independent scoring determinations.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between CCP, CCA, and Lead Assessor roles on an assessment team?
flashcards.answer: CCP assists. CCA assesses and makes scoring determinations. Lead Assessor leads the team, makes final scoring decisions, and submits the final report.

flashcards.question: What is the purpose of the Readiness Review in Phase 1?
flashcards.answer: To verify the OSC is actually prepared before the formal assessment begins. If the OSC isn't ready, the assessment does not proceed.

flashcards.question: What must be included in a CMMC Assessment Plan?
flashcards.answer: Assessment scope, practices being assessed, methodology, logistics, team roles, and how evidence will be collected and handled.

flashcards.question: How are practices on a POA&M scored during Phase 2?
flashcards.answer: NOT MET. POA&M evaluation happens in Phase 4, not during the conduct phase. Do not conflate the two.

flashcards.question: What is the assessment report submission chain?
flashcards.answer: Lead Assessor drafts findings → preliminary results delivered to OSC → Lead Assessor submits to C3PAO and OSC → C3PAO reviews → results submitted to SPRS.

flashcards.question: Why is assessment evidence archived and what role does hashing play?
flashcards.answer: Archived to support any future questions about the assessment. Hashing creates cryptographic fingerprints at time of collection — proves evidence integrity if anyone later questions whether it was altered.

flashcards.question: What three criteria determine whether a NOT MET practice qualifies for a POA&M?
flashcards.answer: 1. OSC meets minimum score threshold. 2. The item is remediable — a fix exists and is achievable. 3. POA&M includes specific milestones showing real progress.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between a POA&M and an operational plan of action?
flashcards.answer: POA&M is associated with a formal CMMC assessment — created when practices score NOT MET, reviewed by the team in Phase 4. Operational plan of action is the OSC's internal document for managing temporary deficiencies outside of a formal assessment.

flashcards.question: What is conditional certification and why does it exist?
flashcards.answer: Allows an OSC to operate while remediating NOT MET practices rather than failing outright and losing contract eligibility. It is a safety valve, not a loophole — the OSC must still remediate within defined timeframes.

flashcards.question: At what two points does the OSC receive visibility into assessment findings?
flashcards.answer: 1. Preliminary results before the final report is submitted. 2. The final assessment report after submission.

flashcards.question: What makes a POA&M item non-qualifying?
flashcards.answer: If the item is not remediable — a structural gap with no viable path to remediation. Vague milestones without real progress also disqualify an item.

flashcards.question: Who submits the final assessment report and to whom?
flashcards.answer: The Lead Assessor submits to both the C3PAO and the OSC. The C3PAO reviews before results are submitted to SPRS.

flashcards.question: What happens during Phase 2 evidence collection and what is the CCP's specific role?
flashcards.answer: Evidence gathered using Examine, Interview, and Test methods for each practice. CCP assists in collecting and examining evidence — pulling documentation, sitting in on interviews, observing demonstrations. Cannot make independent scoring calls.
